summaryTemplate: {{className}} is the {{patternRole}}. A single creation method dispatches between concrete product types based on input, and returns the chosen product through a common type. Pattern body lives at {{file}}:{{lineStart}}-{{lineEnd}}.
chosenRationaleTemplate: Tagged as Factory because {{className}} centralises product selection: the creation method (lines {{lineStart}}-{{lineEnd}}) contains an explicit branching step, the branch bodies instantiate distinct concrete types, and all branches funnel into a return that exposes only the abstract product. The caller never names the concrete class — that's the Factory invariant the detector keys on.

factory_class.note: Factory class boundary — the single point where the decision "which concrete product" is owned. Callers depend on this class instead of on individual product constructors.

factory_branch_decision.note: Selection branch — the input here is the user's intent expressed as a discriminator (kind, type, name). The Factory's job is to map that discriminator to a concrete class without exposing it to the caller.

factory_concrete_creation.note: Concrete product construction — this is where the abstraction leaks intentionally: only inside the Factory does the codebase mention the specific concrete type. Anywhere else the type is referenced through the abstract product.

factory_return.note: Return point — emits the concrete product as the abstract product type. Replacing the return type with the concrete class would break the Factory invariant; callers would couple to specifics.

runnerUpComparisonTemplates.Builder: Builder accumulates state across multiple step calls before emitting a product. {{className}} produces in a single call with branching (lines {{lineStart}}-{{lineEnd}}); there is no per-step state.
runnerUpComparisonTemplates.Singleton: Singleton restricts a class to a single shared instance. {{className}} hands out a fresh product per call; the constraint is on *which class* to instantiate, not on uniqueness.
